creator: william88@gmail.com
text:
On Tue, Mar 5, 2013 at 3:48 PM, Ruslan Makhmatkhanov <cvs-src@yandex.ru>wrote:

> William Grzybowski wrote on 05.03.2013 22:23:
>
>  On Tue, Mar 5, 2013 at 3:12 PM, Ruslan Makhmatkhanov <cvs-src@yandex.ru
>> >wrote:
>>
>>  Hi William,
>>>
>>> could you please list the ports it breaks? 2.0 - is indeed python3 only,
>>> but 2.1 (that is in ports) is both for python2 and python3 (via py-six).
>>> All the "breakages" were not API breakages but rather artificial
>>> limitations of supported dateutil version in package's setup.py and/or
>>> port's Makefile.
>>>
>>>
>> Well, there are several packages that explictly requires dateutil>=1.5 and
>> < 2.0 via setup.py. py-sanetime, py-rhodecode and py-celery are the ones
>> that come to mind right now, but I'm sure there are more.
>> I figured it would be a lot easier to rebocopy dateutil than patch dozens
>> of setup.py files in ports.
>>
>
> Sorry, but I can't agree. I believe that holding one version of this
> library for both python versions is more correctly ideologically, so we
> should patch and report upstream if everything works as expected with 2.1.
>
> As for ports you listed:
>
> a) py-sanetime is patched to not require 1.5 exactly
> b) py-rhodecode is already patching setup.py to align dependency versions,
> so what the problem to introduce another one line for dateutil?
>
> And as I understand, this limitation was introduced in 1.1.8 (in 2011!):
> - fixed #140 freeze of python dateutil library, since new version is
>   python2.x incompatible
>
> So I think just pinging upstream if it works for you will be enough (they
> may be just unaware that they may use 2.1).
>
> c) py-celery is using this construction:
>
> if is_py3k:
>     install_requires.append("**python-dateutil>=2.0")
> else:
>     install_requires.append("**python-dateutil>=1.5,<2.0")
>
> So the upstream just unaware of the 2.1 - it may be tested and reported
> upstream if it works. Sure, they will be glad to remove one layer of
> complexity from their code.
>
> Sorry if I oversimplify the things, but I really believe they are simple
> in that case and that temporary patching/upstream reporting is the way to
> go.


Well, those were just examples, you did not have to go through each one of
them, I understand ;)

While I don't agree I will follow your advice. Please feel free to close
this PR.

Thank you.



-- 
William Grzybowski
------------------------------------------
Agência Livre - www.agencialivre.com.br
Curitiba/PR - Brasil
